diagrams.title: CPU Scheduling Decision Points
diagrams.description: Shows the moments when CPU scheduler makes decisions to switch between processes
elements: CPU Idle - Scheduler selects next process
Process exits - Scheduler selects next process
Process waits for I/O - Scheduler selects next process
Process time slice expires - Scheduler selects next process
Higher priority process arrives - Scheduler may preempt current process
diagrams.purpose: Students understand when scheduling decisions are made and the preemptive vs non-preemptive nature of different algorithms

diagrams.title: Gantt Chart Comparison - FCFS vs SJF vs Round Robin
diagrams.description: Side-by-side Gantt charts showing process execution timelines for same set of processes
elements: Gantt Chart 1 (FCFS): P1(0-8) → P2(8-12) → P3(12-20), Process boxes with burst times labeled, Timeline axis with time units
Gantt Chart 2 (SJF): P2(0-4) → P3(4-12) → P1(12-20), Sorted by shortest burst time, Different color for each process
Gantt Chart 3 (RR Quantum=4): P1(0-4) → P2(4-8) → P3(8-12) → P1(12-16) → P3(16-20), Shows context switches, circular pattern
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize how same processes execute differently under each algorithm and calculate waiting/turnaround times

diagrams.title: Process State Transition with Scheduling
diagrams.description: Process lifecycle showing when scheduling occurs at each state transition
elements: New State - Process created, enters ready queue
Ready State - Process in ready queue, waiting for CPU, Arrow to Running
Running State - CPU executing process, Arrow back to Ready (time slice expiry/preempt), Arrow to Waiting (I/O request), Arrow to Terminated
Waiting State - Process waiting for I/O or event, Arrow to Ready when condition satisfied
diagrams.purpose: Students connect scheduling decisions to process state transitions and understand preemptive scheduling mechanism

diagrams.title: Ready Queue Structures for Different Algorithms
diagrams.description: Shows how ready queue is organized for each scheduling algorithm
elements: FCFS Queue: Linear queue representation [P1]→[P2]→[P3]→[P4], FIFO insertion order, Single-ended arrow showing FIFO processing
Priority Queue: Hierarchical structure with multiple levels, High Priority level [P1, P3], Low Priority level [P2, P4], Arrow showing highest priority selected first
SJF Queue: Arranged by burst time ascending, [P2(BT=3)]→[P1(BT=5)]→[P3(BT=7)], Visual emphasis on shortest at front
Round Robin Queue: Circular/cyclic arrangement, P1→P2→P3→P4→P1..., Shows quantum allocation cycle, Time quantum indicator
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how data structure choice affects scheduling behavior and complexity

charts.title: Scheduling Algorithm Comparison Matrix
charts.content: FCFS: Non-preemptive, Simple FIFO queue, High throughput for long processes, Suffers convoy effect, Avg Wait: High for short processes | SJF: Can be preemptive/non-preemptive, Selects shortest burst, Optimal average waiting time, Suffers from starvation, Difficulty: Requires burst time prediction | Priority: Can be preemptive/non-preemptive, Based on priority value (lower number = higher priority), May cause starvation, Requires aging to prevent starvation | Round Robin: Preemptive with time quantum, Fair allocation, Good response time, Performance depends on quantum size, Throughput affected by context switches | Multilevel Queue: Multiple queues with different algorithms, Real-time processes in separate queue, System processes get priority, Complex but flexible | Multilevel Feedback Queue: Processes can move between queues, Aging prevents starvation, Most general and complex, Used in modern OS

charts.title: Scheduler Selection Decision Tree
charts.content: Start → Is system interactive? → Yes → Is response time critical? → Yes → Use Round Robin | No → Use Multilevel Feedback Queue | No → Is system batch processing? → Yes → Use FCFS or SJF | No → Is there priority requirements? → Yes → Use Priority Scheduling with aging | No → Use FCFS with time slicing | End nodes for each decision path with algorithm names

charts.title: Performance Metrics Calculation Formula
charts.content: Throughput = Number of processes completed / Total time | CPU Utilization = (Busy time / Total time) × 100% | Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time | Waiting Time = Turnaround Time - Burst Time | Response Time = First CPU time - Arrival Time | Efficiency = 1 / (1 - ρ) where ρ = average burst time / (burst time + context switch time)

charts.title: Classification of CPU Scheduling Algorithms
charts.content: Based on Preemption: Non-preemptive (FCFS, SJF non-preemptive, Priority non-preemptive) vs Preemptive (RR, SJF preemptive, Priority preemptive, Multilevel) | Based on Queue Structure: Single queue (FCFS, SJF, Priority, RR) vs Multiple queue (Multilevel Queue, Multilevel Feedback Queue) | Based on Execution Pattern: Batch (FCFS, SJF) vs Interactive (RR, Priority) vs Real-time (Rate Monotonic, EDF)

branches: Scheduling Criteria (CPU utilization, throughput, turnaround, waiting, response)
Algorithm Types (FCFS, SJF, Priority, Round Robin)
Queue Structures (Single queue, Multiple queue, Feedback queue)
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of CPU scheduling algorithms for exam preparation and conceptual clarity
